packaging.title: Why Airlines Overbook Every Flight
altTitles: How Airlines Sell Seats That Don't Exist
The Real Reason You Got Bumped
packaging.description: Inside airline yield management, why carriers deliberately sell more tickets than seats, and the statistical models that decide who gets bumped and who flies.

thumbnail.visualIdea: a single airplane seat with multiple boarding passes stacked on it

packaging.hookPromise: Airlines sell more tickets than there are seats on purpose, and a hidden algorithm decides every day exactly how many passengers to leave at the gate.
openLoops: Why would an airline sell a seat it doesn't have?
How does the system decide who gets bumped?
Why is the same seat priced ten different ways?
packaging.payoff: Airlines run no-show prediction models so precise that overbooking earns more than the rare payout to bumped passengers — the empty-seat loss is a bigger fear than the bumping cost.
packaging.whyItWorks: Flying is a shared frustration and 'they sold a seat that doesn't exist' is an irresistible insider mechanism.
